content:
Memory hierarchy inversion and streaming layers for model training without massive hardware clusters

6 min read
Just now

--

GPU VRAM capacity has been a very strong filter in the field of Large language model training for a long time. Over time, we’ve seen sophisticated offloading techniques but even with them, developers hit hard limits when scaling beyond tens of billions of parameters on single devices. Today we’re discussing a novel approach called MegaTrain which tries to change this equation entirely by treating the GPU as a transient compute cache.
The system stores parameters, gradients, and optimizer states in host CPU memory while streaming only the active layers to the GPU on demand. This approach allows reliable training of models up to 120 billion parameters on a single H200 GPU paired with 1.5 TB of DDR5 memory. We’ll also look into systems design, memory management, and high-performance computing that extend far beyond this specific implementation. Let’s get started.
The Traditional Memory Bottleneck in LLM Training
Modern LLM training, especially post-training phases like instruction tuning, domain adaptation, and alignment, are more memory-bound than they’re compute-bound. Full-precision or mixed-precision setups require storage for model weights, gradients, optimizer states and activations. For a 100B+ parameter model, this footprint quickly exceeds even high-end GPU VRAM.
